title: India’s Expanding SSBN Force and What It Means for Pakistan
gist: India's SSBN fleet is expanding from three operational boats toward a fourth in 2027 and a new S5-class, raising unresolved questions about whether India will pursue continuous at-sea deterrence or a bastion strategy and which command controls the submarines — with direct implications for the India-Pakistan deterrence balance.

insights.claim: India currently operates three commissioned nuclear-powered ballistic missile submarines (SSBNs) — INS Arihant, INS Arighaat, and INS Aridhaman — with a fourth, INS Arisudan, set to join the fleet in 2027.
insights.speaker: Usman Haider
insights.so_what: Track the INS Arisudan 2027 commissioning as a concrete near-term milestone in India's sea-based deterrent buildout.
insights.locator: Currently, India has three operational SSBNs

insights.claim: After completing four Arihant-class submarines, India will move to the next phase of its SSBN program by building S5-class submarines.
insights.speaker: Usman Haider
insights.so_what: The S5-class program is the next capability step to watch for signs of a larger, more capable Indian sea-based deterrent.

insights.claim: India's growing SSBN program raises the open question of whether New Delhi will pursue continuous at-sea deterrence (CASD) or instead follow the Russian-originated bastion strategy of keeping submarines in protected home waters.
insights.speaker: Usman Haider
insights.so_what: India's choice between CASD and a bastion posture will shape submarine basing, escort requirements, and the credibility of its second-strike capability — a key signal to monitor in Indian naval doctrine statements.
insights.locator: Will India pursue continuous at-sea deterrence

insights.claim: It remains unresolved whether operational control over India's SSBN fleet will rest with the Indian Navy or with the Strategic Forces Command (SFC).
insights.speaker: Usman Haider
insights.so_what: Command-and-control arrangements affect crisis-stability and escalation-control dynamics with Pakistan; watch for formal SFC-Navy arrangements as a signal of how India intends to employ these assets.
insights.locator: Who will maintain control over these submarines

insights.claim: The growth of India's SSBN fleet directly affects the nuclear deterrence equation between India and Pakistan.
insights.speaker: Usman Haider
insights.so_what: An expanding Indian sea-based second-strike capability is likely to pressure Pakistan toward its own countermeasures, a factor relevant to South Asian strategic-stability risk assessments.
insights.locator: how does the growth of India's SSBN count affect the deterrence equation
